24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
160	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
161	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
163	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
164	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
165	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
166	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
167	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
168	  %  the function
169	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
170	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
171	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
172	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
174	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
175	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
176	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
177	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
178	  \[
179	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
180	  \]
181	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
182	  the tensor product of sections
183	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
184	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
185	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
186	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
187	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
188	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
189	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
190	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
191	  polynomial ring).
192	\end{definition}
193	
194	\begin{definition}
195	[Section graded module]
196	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
198	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
199	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
200	  % Polynomials" (read from
201	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
204	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
205	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
206	  %  the function
207	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
208	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
209	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
210	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
212	  I.7.}
213	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
214	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
215	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
216	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
217	  \[
218	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
219	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
220	  \]
221	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
222	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
223	  by the section tensor product
224	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
225	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
226	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
227	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
228	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
229	\end{definition}
230	
231	\begin{lemma}
232	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
233	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
235	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
236	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
237	  % Polynomials" (read from
238	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
239	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
240	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
241	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
242	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
243	  %  the function
244	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
245	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
246	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
247	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
248	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
249	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
250	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
251	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
252	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
253	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
254	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
255	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
256	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
257	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{proof}
261	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
262	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
263	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
264	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
265	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
266	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
267	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
268	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
269	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
270	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
271	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
272	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
273	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
274	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
275	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
279	  graded series]
280	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
281	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
282	  \mathlibok
283	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
284	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
285	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
286	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
287	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
288	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
289	  equals \(h(n)\):
290	  \[
291	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
292	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
293	  \]
294	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
295	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
296	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
297	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
298	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
299	\end{lemma}
300	
301	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
302	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
304	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
305	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
306	  % Polynomials" (read from
307	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
308	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
309	  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
310	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
311	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
312	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
313	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
314	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
315	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
316	  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
317	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
318	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
319	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
320	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
321	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
322	  such that for all \(n > N\)
323	  \[
324	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
325	  \]
326	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
327	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
328	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
329	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
330	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
331	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
332	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
333	
334	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
335	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
336	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
337	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
338	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
339	  rational series from the module.
340	\end{lemma}
341	
342	\begin{proof}
343	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
344	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
345	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
346	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
347	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
348	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
349	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
350	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
351	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
352	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
353	  graded modules
354	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
355	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
356	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
357	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
358	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
359	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
360	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
361	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
362	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
363	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
364	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
368	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
370	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
371	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
372	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
373	  % Polynomials" (read from
374	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
375	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
376	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
377	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
378	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
379	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
380	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
381	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
382	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
383	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
384	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
385	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
386	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
387	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
388	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
389	  \[
390	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
391	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
392	  \]
393	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
394	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
395	\end{theorem}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
399	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
400	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
401	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
402	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
403	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
404	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
405	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
406	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
407	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
408	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
409	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
410	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
411	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
412	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
413	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
414	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
415	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
416	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
417	\end{proof}
418	
419	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
420	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
421	
422	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
423	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
424	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
425	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
426	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
427	
428	\begin{definition}
429	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
430	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
432	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
433	  % Polynomials" (read from
434	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
435	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
436	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
437	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
438	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
439	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
440	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
441	  project-built primitive.}
442	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a coherent sheaf of
443	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\)
444	  is the ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for
445	  each affine open \(U \subseteq X\),
446	  \[
447	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
448	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
449	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
450	  \]
451	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
452	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
453	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
454	
455	  \emph{Note.} This is a project-built primitive; Mathlib does not carry an
456	  annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
457	\end{definition}
458	
459	\begin{definition}
460	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
461	  \label{def:schematic_support}
462	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
463	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
464	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
465	  % Polynomials" (read from
466	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
467	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
468	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
469	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
470	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
471	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
472	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
473	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
474	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
475	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
476	  \[
477	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
478	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
479	  \]
480	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
481	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
482	  \(X\).
483	\end{definition}
484	
485	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
486	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
487	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
488	  \mathlibok
489	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
490	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
491	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
492	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
493	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
494	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
495	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
496	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
497	\end{lemma}
498	
499	\begin{definition}
500	[Proper support over the base]
501	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
502	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
503	  \uses{def:schematic_support, lem:isProper_mathlib}
504	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
505	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
506	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
507	  % + L418-L419).
508	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
509	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
510	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
511	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
512	  %  [...]
513	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
514	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
515	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
516	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
517	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
518	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
519	  \[
520	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
521	  \]
522	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
523	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
524	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
525	  functor's pullback action requires.
526	\end{definition}
527	
528	\begin{definition}
529	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
530	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
531	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
532	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises (2)" and
533	  % "Grassmannian of a vector bundle" (read from
534	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L562-L564
535	  % + L584-L585).
536	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
537	  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
538	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
539	  %  [...]
540	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
541	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,"
542	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
543	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
544	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
545	  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with
546	  isomorphisms \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus d}\) for
547	  each \(i\).
548	
549	  \emph{Note.} This predicate is project-built; it is not supplied by Mathlib.
550	\end{definition}
551	
552	\section{The Quot functor}
553	\label{sec:quot_functor}
554	
555	\begin{definition}\leanok
556	[Quot functor]
557	  \label{def:quot_functor}
558	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
559	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
560	
561	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
562	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
563	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
564	  % + L481-L498).
565	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
566	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
567	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
568	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
569	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
570	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
571	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
572	  %
573	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
574	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
575	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
576	  %
577	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
578	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
579	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
580	  %
581	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
582	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
583	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
584	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
585	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
586	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
587	  %  well-defined, which gives
588	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
589	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
590	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
591	  %  [...]
592	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
593	  %  as a co-product
594	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
595	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
596	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
597	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
598	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
599	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
600	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
601	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
602	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
603	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
604	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
605	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
606	  \[
607	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
608	  \]
609	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
610	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
611	  \begin{enumerate}
612	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
613	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
614	      which is flat over \(T\);
615	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
616	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
617	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
618	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
619	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
620	      \(\Phi\).
621	  \end{enumerate}
622	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
623	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
624	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
625	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
626	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
627	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
628	\end{definition}
629	
630	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
631	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
632	
633	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
634	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
635	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
636	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
637	
638	\begin{definition}\leanok
639	[Grassmannian scheme]
640	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
642	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
643	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
644	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
645	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
646	  % + L773-L850).
647	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
648	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
649	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
650	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
651	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
652	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
653	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
654	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
655	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
656	  %  contravariant functor
657	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
658	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
659	  %  set of all equivalence classes
660	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
661	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
662	  %  [...]
663	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
664	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
665	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
666	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
667	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
668	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
669	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
670	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
671	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
672	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
673	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
674	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
675	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
676	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
677	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
678	  \[
679	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
680	    \qquad
681	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
682	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
683	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
684	  \]
685	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
686	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
687	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
688	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
689	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
690	\end{definition}
691	
692	\begin{lemma}[Representability predicate]
693	  \label{lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}
694	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRepresentable}
695	  \mathlibok
696	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
697	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and \(F : \mathcal{C}^{op} \to \mathbf{Set}\)
698	  a presheaf of sets. The predicate \(F.\mathrm{IsRepresentable}\) asserts that
699	  \(F\) is representable, i.e.\ that there exists an object \(Y\) of
700	  \(\mathcal{C}\) together with a natural bijection
701	  \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-, Y) \cong F\) (the Yoneda bijection).
702	\end{lemma}
703

705	[Representability of the Grassmannian]

724	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together

726	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
727	  % L798-L930).
728	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
729	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), the Grassmannian scheme
730	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
731	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
732	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
733	  %  can be explicitly constructed as follows. [...]
734	  %  the schemes
735	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the
736	  %  \({r\choose d}\) different
737	  %  subsets of  \(\{ 1,\ldots, r\}\) of cardinality \(d\),
738	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle \((\theta_{I,J})\) to form a
739	  %  finite-type scheme \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\). As each \(U^I\) is
740	  %  isomorphic to \(\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}\), it follows that
741	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
742	  %  [...]
743	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
744	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
745	  %  [...]
746	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
747	  %  the determinant line bundle
748	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
749	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
750	  %  [...]
751	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
752	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
753	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
754	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
755	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
756	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
757	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
758	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
759	  Explained Ch.~5).}
760	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
761	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
762	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
763	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
764	  equipped with a tautological quotient
765	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
766	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
767	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
768	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
769	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
770	
771	\end{theorem}
772	
773	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
774	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
775	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
776	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
777	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
778	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
779	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
780	\begin{proof}
781	  
782	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
783	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
784	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
785	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
786	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
787	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
788	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
789	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
790	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
791	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
792	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
793	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
794	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
795	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
796	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
797	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
798	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
799	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
800	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
801	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
802	
803	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
804	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
805	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
806	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
807	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
808	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
809	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
810	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
811	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
812	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
813	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
814	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
815	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
816	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
817	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
818	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
819	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
820	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
821	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
822	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
823	\end{proof}
824	
825	\section{Out of scope}
826	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
827	
828	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
829	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
830	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
831	\begin{itemize}
832	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
833	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton
834	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,
835	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.
836	  \item \textbf{Flat base change of cohomology} (Stacks 02KH) and its
837	    project-side substrate -- not constructed in this chapter.
838	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
839	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
840	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
841	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
842	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
843	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
844	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
845	    project-side sub-build for the Lean encoding.
846	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
847	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
848	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
849	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
850	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
851	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
852	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme
853	    structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)); that assembly lives in the parent project
854	    which consumes this Quot-foundations layer and is not part of the present
855	    subproject.
856	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
857	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
858	    constructed in this chapter.
859	\end{itemize}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`

76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  Constructed directly in Lean as the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); this is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

184	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
185	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}

188	  % \uses. The universal property is the PRIMITIVE statement, proved by reducing

204	  % a functor'', functor F and Lemma lemma-spec

207	  % "For any \(f : T \to S\) the pullback
208	  %  \(f^*\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras.
209	  %  We are going to consider pairs \((f : T \to S, \varphi)\) where

214	  %  Sheaves, Lemma REF.
215	  %  This is also the same as giving an \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra map
216	  %  \(\varphi : \mathcal{A} \to f_*\mathcal{O}_T\), see
217	  %  Sheaves, Lemma REF.
218	  %  We will use all three ways of thinking about \(\varphi\),
219	  %  without further mention. [...]
220	  %  In this way we have defined a functor
221	  %  \begin{eqnarray}
222	  %  \label{equation-spec}
223	  %  F : \Sch^{opp} & \longrightarrow & \textit{Sets} \\
224	  %  T & \longmapsto & F(T) = \{\text{pairs }(f, \varphi) \text{ as above}\}
225	  %  \nonumber
226	  %  \end{eqnarray}
227	  %  [...]
228	  %  The functor \(F\) is representable by a scheme."
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
230	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
231	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
232	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
233	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
234	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
235	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$
236	  Equivalently, for any \(X\)-scheme \(g : T \to X\),
237	  there is a natural bijection
238	  \[
239	    \Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \;\;\cong\;\;
240	    \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_* \mathcal{O}_T)
241	  \]
242	  (using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between
243	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
244	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).
245	\end{theorem}

247	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: TODO retrieve from references/stacks-constructions.tex
248	% (proof of lemma-spec, L553-L600, ~50 lines covering the Zariski sheaf property,

250	% with the affine-base case via lemma-spec-base-change and lemma-spec-affine).
251	% Verbatim quote omitted for length; the structural steps are restated below in
252	% project notation.
253	\begin{proof}
254	  
255	  \leanok
256	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.

268	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
269	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
270	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
271	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
272	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
273	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
274	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).
275	  Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
276	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
277	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
278	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
279	  of \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
280	\end{proof}

299	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

305	  Let \(X = \Spec R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
306	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
307	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
308	  \[
309	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
310	  \]
311	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
312	  spectrum of the global sections.
313	\end{theorem}

328	% \varphi_{univ} :

330	% \longrightarrow
331	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
332	% \(\)
333	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),

335	% \((f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})\) represents \(F\) in this case."

338	  \leanok
339	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
340	  % The iter-176 ``change IsAffine (Spec R); inferInstance`` form is now stale
341	  % — iter-179 Lane B closes via ``UniversalProperty 𝒜`` + ``isAffine_of_isAffineHom``
342	  % against the iter-179 Mathlib-backed ``RelativeSpec``/``structureMorphism``
343	  % bodies. The Lean type remains the strictly weaker ``IsAffine`` rather than

345	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).
346	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
347	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
348	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on
349	  \(\widetilde A\)). The ring map \(R \to A\) gives a morphism
350	  \(f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X\) and a canonical
351	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map
352	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
353	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
354	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
355	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
356	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
357	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
358	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
359	  is a ring map \(A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and hence corresponds to a morphism
360	  \(T \to \Spec A\) by the affine universal property
361	  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
362	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
363	  \((f, \varphi) \mapsto a\) defined by \(f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a\),
364	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).
365	\end{proof}

367	\section{Lean encoding}
368	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
369	
370	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
371	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
372	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
373	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
374	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
375	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
376	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
377	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
378	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
379	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
380	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
381	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
382	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
383	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
384	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
385	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
386	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
387	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
388	infrastructure.
389

393	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its core

406	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.

1190	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1191	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1193	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1194	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1195	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1196	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1197	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1198	\end{lemma}
1199	\begin{proof}
1200	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1201	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1202	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1203	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1204	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1205	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1206	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1207	\end{proof}

1210	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1211	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1213	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1214	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1215	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1216	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1217	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1218	\end{lemma}
1219	\begin{proof}
1220	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1221	  \cref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1222	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1223	\end{proof}

1238	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1239	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1240	  % L933--938).
1241	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1242	  % equality
1243	  % $$
1244	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1245	  % $$
1246	  % as $R'$-modules."
1247	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1248	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1249	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1250	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1251	  \[
1252	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1253	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1254	  \]
1255	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1256	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1257	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1258	  hypothesis.
1259	\end{lemma}
1260	
1261	\begin{proof}
1262	  \uses{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib, lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
1263	  The underlying additive bijection is supplied by the standalone, geometry-free
1264	  \(R'\)-linear equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}; the work
1265	  of this proof is to repackage that equivalence as a morphism of \(R'\)-module objects.
1266	  Two points of bookkeeping make the repackaging non-trivial, and pinning them down
1267	  \emph{is} the content of the proof. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1268	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1269	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1270	
1271	  \emph{The two \(A\)-actions are distinct types, so an identity bridge is mandatory.}
1272	  The source of the standalone equivalence, \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), is the
1273	  relative tensor product over the \emph{canonical} \(A\)-action on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1274	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the
1275	  carrier object produced by the dictionaries is
1276	  \((\operatorname{extendScalars}\iota)\,M\) with \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) the
1277	  left inclusion, whose relative tensor product is taken over the
1278	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\iota\) \(A\)-action. These two \(A\)-module structures on
1279	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) coincide on elements, but the tensor product depends on the
1280	  \(A\)-module structure in its type, so the two tensor products are \emph{not equal}
1281	  even though their underlying abelian groups are the same. One therefore inserts the identity \(A\)-linear bridge
1282	  \[
1283	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M
1284	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1285	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M ,
1286	  \]
1287	  which re-reads the same generators against the carrier's \(A\)-action. This bridge is
1288	  the mathematically essential glue between the standalone helper and the object; it
1289	  cannot be elided.
1290	
1291	  \emph{The residue is the \(R'\)-linearity of the assembled additive map, checked on
1292	  generators.} After the bridge, the only remaining obligation is that the assembled
1293	  additive equivalence respects the \(R'\)-module structures. The \(R'\)-action on the
1294	  carrier is restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1295	  \(r' \mapsto 1 \otimes r'\), so \(r' \cdot y = (1 \otimes r') \cdot y\). On a
1296	  generator the extension-of-scalars action then computes
1297	  \[
1298	    (1 \otimes r') \cdot \bigl((a \otimes s) \otimes m\bigr)
1299	    = \bigl((1 \otimes r')(a \otimes s)\bigr) \otimes m
1300	    = \bigl(a \otimes (r' s)\bigr) \otimes m ,
1301	  \]
1302	  using \((1 \otimes r')(a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\) in \(A \otimes_R R'\). The
1303	  helper's underlying map sends \((a \otimes s) \otimes m \mapsto s \otimes (a \cdot m)\)
1304	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}), so it carries
1305	  \(\bigl(a \otimes (r' s)\bigr) \otimes m\) to
1306	  \((r' s) \otimes (a \cdot m) = r' \cdot \bigl(s \otimes (a \cdot m)\bigr)\), which is
1307	  \(r'\) times the image of the original generator. Thus the additive map commutes with
1308	  the \(R'\)-action on generators, hence everywhere by bilinearity; together with the
1309	  bridge \(eT\) this promotes the standalone equivalence to the desired bundled
1310	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism. Being a composite of linear equivalences the result is an
1311	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents carries a flatness hypothesis.
1312	\end{proof}

1314	% --- Generator-trace decomposition (the three Mathlib-absent plumbing steps of the
1315	%     adjoint-mate computation, isolated as well-typed pieces): unit value ←
1316	%     pseudofunctor reindex ← (g^* ⊣ g_*) transpose-on-elements formula. ---

1318	\begin{lemma}
1319	[Unit value of the affine base-change adjunction on a generator]
1320	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1321	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_unit_value}
1322	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
1323	  In the affine--affine model, let \(\eta'\) be the unit of the
1324	  \(\bigl((g')^*, (g')_*\bigr)\)-adjunction along the projection
1325	  \(g' = \operatorname{pullback.fst}\). Read through the codomain dictionary
1326	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}, the
1327	  component \(\eta'_{\widetilde M}\) on the global \(A\)-module \(M\) is the
1328	  base-change unit
1329	  \[
1330	    m \;\longmapsto\; (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m \;\in\; (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M .
1331	  \]
1332	\end{lemma}
1333	
1334	\begin{proof}
1335	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
1336	  The unit of an extension-of-scalars adjunction sends \(m\) to the elementary tensor
1337	  \(1 \otimes m\). Under the pullback dictionary
1338	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, naturality at the unit identifies
1339	  \((g')^*\widetilde M\) on global sections with the extension of scalars
1340	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) along \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\) and carries the unit
1341	  element \(1 \otimes m\) to \((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\). Composing with the codomain
1342	  read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}
1343	  leaves the value \((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) on the generator \(m\).
1344	\end{proof}
1345	
1346	\begin{lemma}
1347	[Pseudofunctor reindexing of the pushforward target]
1348	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}
1349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}
1350	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1351	  In the affine--affine model, applying \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) to the
1352	  unit assignment \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of
1353	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value} leaves it unchanged on elements
1354	  (restriction of scalars is the identity on underlying sets), and the pseudofunctor
1355	  identities \((g'f)_* = (f'g)_* = g_*\,f'_*\) (forced by the commutativity
1356	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square) reindex the target as the \(R'\)-module
1357	  \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) of the codomain read
1358	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
1359	\end{lemma}
1360	
1361	\begin{proof}
1362	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1363	  Restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) does not move elements, so \(f_*\) carries
1364	  the assignment \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) to itself on underlying sets.
1365	  The coherence of the pushforward pseudofunctor under the equality
1366	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) — composition of pushforwards together with congruence
1367	  under equal morphisms — rewrites \((g'f)_*\widetilde M\) as
1368	  \(g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\), whose inner object
1369	  \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) is read by
1370	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} as \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1371	  This reindexing is exactly the in-file pushforward-composition and
1372	  pushforward-congruence naturality applied to the codomain dictionary; no new content
1373	  beyond those category-level identities is needed.
1374	\end{proof}

1376	\begin{lemma}
1377	[Transpose-on-elements formula for the base-change adjunction]
1378	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}
1379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}
1380	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1381	  % NOTE: the (g^* ⊣ g_*) adjunction here is the affine base-change /
1382	  % extension-restriction adjunction along ψ : R → R'; the formula below is the
1383	  % standard transpose-on-elements identity for an extension-of-scalars adjunction,
1384	  % so it stands on its proof sketch with no external reference.
1385	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\). For an \(R\)-linear map
1386	  \(u : \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M \to \operatorname{restr}_\psi N\), the
1387	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose \(\widehat u\) is the unique \(R'\)-linear map
1388	  \(\widehat u : R' \otimes_R M \to N\) determined on generators by
1389	  \(\widehat u(r' \otimes m) = r' \cdot u(m)\). Applied to the unit assignment
1390	  \(u : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of
1391	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}, with the source read as
1392	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}, the transpose is
1393	  \[
1394	    r' \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
1395	    \;=\; (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m ,
1396	  \]
1397	  the \(R'\)-action scaling the \(R'\)-tensor factor of \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1398	\end{lemma}
1399	
1400	\begin{proof}
1401	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1402	  The transpose under an extension-of-scalars adjunction
1403	  \(g^* = R' \otimes_R (-)\) is the unique \(R'\)-linear extension of \(u\) along the
1404	  unit \(m \mapsto 1 \otimes m\), so it is determined on generators by
1405	  \(\widehat u(r' \otimes m) = r' \cdot u(m)\). Substituting
1406	  \(u(m) = (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) and using that the \(R'\)-action on
1407	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) scales the \(R'\)-tensor factor
1408	  (\(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\)) yields the stated
1409	  value. The source identification \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the domain read
1410	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}.
1411	\end{proof}

1413	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1414	[Generator identification of the conjugated section-level map]
1415	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace_eq}
1416	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_generator_trace_eq}
1417	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1418	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1419	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma

1421	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1422	  % L933--938).
1423	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1424	  % equality
1425	  % $$

1427	  % $$
1428	  % as $R'$-modules."
1429	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1430	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1431	  With the domain and codomain pinned by Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}
1432	  and Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}, the conjugated section-level
1433	  base-change map
1434	  \[
1435	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} :
1436	    R' \otimes_R M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M
1437	  \]
1438	  sends \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\); equivalently it equals the
1439	  \emph{inverse} of the regrouping isomorphism
1440	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}, i.e.\
1441	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
1442	  = \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\).
1443	\end{lemma}
1444	
1445	\begin{proof}
1446	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1447	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv,
1448	        lem:base_change_mate_unit_value, lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex,
1449	        lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}
1450	  By Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \(\alpha\) is the
1451	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose of \(f_*(\eta')\), with \(\eta'\) the
1452	  \(\bigl((g')^*, (g')_*\bigr)\)-unit. The computation is the thin assembly of three
1453	  isolated steps, each reading the relevant functor on global modules through the
1454	  dictionaries of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} and
1455	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
1456	  \begin{itemize}
1457	    \item[(A)] \emph{The unit value.} By
1458	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}, the \(\bigl((g')^*,(g')_*\bigr)\)-unit
1459	      \(\eta'\), read through \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\), is the base-change unit
1460	      \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1461	    \item[(C)] \emph{Pushforward and reindex.} By
1462	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}, applying
1463	      \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) leaves this assignment unchanged on
1464	      elements and reindexes the target, via the pseudofunctor identities, as the
1465	      codomain read \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1466	    \item[(B)] \emph{Transpose under \((g^* \dashv g_*)\).} By
1467	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}, the transpose of the assignment
1468	      \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) is the \(R'\)-linear map
1469	      \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
1470	      = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\), with source read as \(R' \otimes_R M\) by
1471	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}.
1472	  \end{itemize}
1473	  Composing steps (A), (C), (B) shows that
1474	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\) sends
1475	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). This assignment is exactly the
1476	  inverse generator of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}, so the conjugated
1477	  map agrees with \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\) on generators and hence (both being
1478	  \(R'\)-linear) everywhere. This is exactly the ``boils down to the equality''
1479	  computation of the source.
1480	\end{proof}

1592	  hypothesis, this identification closes the affine case: \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an
1593	  isomorphism, hence (full faithfulness / counit isomorphism of \(\widetilde{(-)}\) on
1594	  quasi-coherent modules) so is \(\alpha\) on the affine chart.

1598	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\) maps induced by \(\psi : R \to R'\) and
1599	  \(\varphi : R \to A\), with \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\),
1600	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), and \(h\) the cartesian-square witness — the
1601	  statement is the equality of \(R'\)-linear maps
1602	  \[
1603	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma\bigl(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\
1604	      f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\ \widetilde{M}\bigr) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
1605	    \;=\;

1611	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, and
