1613	  \xrightarrow{\sim} (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) is assembled from the same two
1614	  dictionaries; equivalently, after pre/post-composing with the dictionary isos,
1615	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) \emph{is} the cancellation isomorphism. The conclusion type is a
1616	  propositional equality of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\)-morphisms (whence
1617	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \Gamma(\alpha)\) and \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \alpha\) follow), not merely
1618	  an abstract-mate identification at the level of sheaves.

1621	\begin{proof}
1622	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1623	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace,
1624	        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1625	  The proof chains the four pieces into which the generator trace has been
1626	  decomposed. By the domain read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} the
1627	  source \(\Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde M)\bigr)\) is
1628	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) via \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\); by the codomain read
1629	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} — which rests on the
1630	  \texttt{pullbackSpecIso} identification of the cone legs
1631	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}, the single Mathlib-absent step —
1632	  the target \(\Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)\) is
1633	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) via \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). By the generator
1634	  trace Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace} the conjugated map
1635	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\) sends
1636	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). By
1637	  \texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul} (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}) this
1638	  assignment is exactly \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\); equivalently, in the
1639	  cancellation direction \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\) is
1640	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\).
1641	
1642	  Since \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is a Mathlib linear equivalence carrying
1643	  \emph{no} flatness hypothesis, the conjugated map is an isomorphism; being the
1644	  conjugate of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) by the two dictionary isomorphisms
1645	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}, \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\), it follows that
1646	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is itself an isomorphism. This closes the affine case
1647	  \emph{directly} on global sections, with no separate abstract-mate-versus-concrete-map
1648	  identification at the level of sheaves. (The full equality
1649	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
1650	  = \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\) of the intended signature follows from the
1651	  same chain once \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}, \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) are formed; the
1652	  formalized decl records the \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \Gamma(\alpha)\) corollary, which is
1653	  what the affine close consumes.)
1654	\end{proof}

1664	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma

1678	  change''.}

1681	  above the canonical map of
1682	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism

1712	% NOTE: This affine lemma is closed directly on global sections, via the two named
1713	% obligations: lem:base_change_map_affine_local (locality reduction on the affine
1714	% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-
1715	% level value of the base-change map is cancelBaseChange). Both proved affine tilde
1716	% dictionaries -- lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso and lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso --

1719	\begin{proof}
1720	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
1721	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
1722	  global sections}.
1723	
1724	  \medskip
1725	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
1726	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
1727	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
1728	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
1729	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
1730	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
1731	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
1732	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
1733	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
1734	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
1735	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
1736	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
1737	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
1738	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
1739	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
1740	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
1741	
1742	  \medskip
1743	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
1744	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
1745	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
1746	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
1747	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
1748	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
1749	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
1750	  \[
1751	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
1752	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
1753	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
1754	  \]
1755	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
1756	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
1757	  of modules.
1758	
1759	  \medskip
1760	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
1761	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
1762	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
1763	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
1764	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
1765	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
1766	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
1767	  \[
1768	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1769	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
1770	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
1771	  \]
1772	  of \(R'\)-modules (Mathlib's
1773	  \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). This is the entire
1774	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
1775	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
1776	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
1777	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
1778	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
1779	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
1780	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
1781	  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).
1782	\end{proof}

1784	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
1785	
1786	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
1787	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
1788	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.

1790	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
1791	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1792	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
1793	  \mathlibok
1794	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1795	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
1796	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
1797	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
1798	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
1799	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
1800	  \[
1801	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
1802	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1803	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
1804	  \]
1805	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
1806	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
1807	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
1808	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
1809	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
1810	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
1811	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
1812	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
1813	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
1814	\end{lemma}

1846	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
1847	  \(i = 0\) case.}
1848	  Consider the cartesian square
1849	  \[
1850	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1851	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
1852	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
1853	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
1854	    \end{array}
1855	  \]
1856	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
1857	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
1858	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
1859	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
1860	  \[
1861	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1862	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
1863	  \]

1865	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
1866	  \[
1867	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1868	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
1869	  \]
1870	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
1871	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
1872	  \(\mathcal{F}\).

1874	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
1875	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of

1877	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
1878	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
1879	  an isomorphism.

1948	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1949	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
1950	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
1951	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
1952	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
1953	  sequence.

1955	  \medskip
1956	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
1957	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
1958	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
1959	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
1960	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
1961	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
1962	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
1963	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
1964	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
1965	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
1966	  \[
1967	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
1968	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
1969	  \]
1970	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
1971	
1972	  \medskip
1973	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
1974	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
1975	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
1976	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
1977	  \[
1978	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
1979	    \;=\;
1980	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
1981	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
1982	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
1983	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
1984	    \Bigr),
1985	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
1986	  \]
1987	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
1988	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
1989	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
1990	  higher cohomology.
1991	
1992	  \medskip
1993	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
1994	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
1995	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
1996	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
1997	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
1998	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
1999	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2000	  \[
2001	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2002	    \qquad
2003	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2004	  \]
2005	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2006	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2007	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2008	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2009	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, i.e.\ Mathlib's
2010	  \texttt{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}):
2011	  \[
2012	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2013	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2014	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2015	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2016	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2017	  \]
2018	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2019	
2020	  \medskip
2021	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2022	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2023	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2024	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2025	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2026	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2027	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2028	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2029	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2030	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2031	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2032	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2033	  \[
2034	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2035	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2036	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2037	  \]
2038	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2039	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2040	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2041	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2042	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2043	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2044	\end{proof}

  cocycle gluing of the cells into a scheme `Gr_S(V,d)` + separatedness via the diagonal
  (~150–350 LOC); **GR-quot** — tautological rank-`d` quotient `π*V ↠ U` + its universal property
  (~100–250 LOC); **GR-repr** — functor-of-points identification `Grass(V,d) ≅ Hom_S(−,Gr)` giving
  `RepresentableBy` (~100–250 LOC). GR-repr needs either `thm:relative_spec_univ` strengthened to a
  `RepresentableBy` witness or a transpose-free gluing argument (Open question).

## Open strategic questions

- RelativeSpec strengthening: `thm:relative_spec_univ` is proved only as `IsAffineHom`, not the
  `RepresentableBy` Yoneda form; `thm:relative_spec_affine_base` only as `IsAffine`, not the
  canonical iso `Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X,𝒜))`. GR-repr needs the stronger forms. Decide: strengthen
  RelativeSpec (re-opens proved work) vs. a RepresentableBy-free GR-repr argument. Deferred —
  QUOT-repr is many iters out.
- Does FBC-A's direct-on-sections route fully dissolve the coherence wall, or does identifying
  `Γ(pushforwardBaseChangeMap)` with the canonical tensor iso still need a mate computation?
  Validate empirically once the affine lemma is attempted.

## Mathlib gaps & new material

Gaps to fill:
- FBC-B: H⁰-as-equalizer / finite-affine-cover sheaf-condition packaging for `SheafOfModules`.
- GF-alg: polynomial-ring generic-freeness residue not covered by `exists_free_localizedModule_powers`.
- SNAP: (a) Serre `⊕ₘΓ(F⊗Lᵐ)` finitely-generated-graded-module correspondence (H⁰ only); (b) graded
  Hilbert–Serre rationality `gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational` (`dim_κ Mₙ` → `p·(1-X)^{-d}`) —
  Mathlib-ABSENT, project build; (c) extraction via the verified `Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly`.
  All Čech-independent — no higher cohomology. (Mathlib provides only (c).)
- QUOT predicates: schematic-support/proper-support; rank-`r` local-freeness for `SheafOfModules`.
- QUOT-repr: Grassmannian-of-quotients as a scheme (Nitsure §1/§5 big-cell patching), per GR-* above.

New project material:
- `genericFlatnessAlgebraic` (new decl), re-signed `genericFlatness` (+`IsQuasicoherent`+`IsFiniteType`).
- Coherence encoding = `[IsQuasicoherent]` + `[IsFiniteType]` over the locally noetherian base
  (no single `IsCoherent` predicate at the pin), used uniformly across GF and QUOT re-signs.
- QUOT defs with tightened signatures (coherence on F/E, locally-free + rank on V, universe pin);
  `Grassmannian` via `QuotFunctor`; representability stated as `IsRepresentable`.

1461	    \item[(C)] \emph{Pushforward and reindex.} By
1462	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}, applying
1463	      \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) leaves this assignment unchanged on
1464	      elements and reindexes the target, via the pseudofunctor identities, as the
1465	      codomain read \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1466	    \item[(B)] \emph{Transpose under \((g^* \dashv g_*)\).} By
1467	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}, the transpose of the assignment
1468	      \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) is the \(R'\)-linear map
1469	      \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
1470	      = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\), with source read as \(R' \otimes_R M\) by
1471	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}.
1472	  \end{itemize}
1473	  Composing steps (A), (C), (B) shows that
1474	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\) sends
1475	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). This assignment is exactly the
1476	  inverse generator of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}, so the conjugated
1477	  map agrees with \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\) on generators and hence (both being
1478	  \(R'\)-linear) everywhere. This is exactly the ``boils down to the equality''
1479	  computation of the source.
1480	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	\leanok
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	  The formal target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic} regards \(M\) as an
65	  \(A\)-module through the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\), and packages the
66	  localisation \(M_f\) as the localised module at the powers of \(f\): there exists
67	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M}
68	  is free over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
69	\end{theorem}
70	
71	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
72	% exactly this signature):
73	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
74	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
75	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
76	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
77	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
78	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
79	
80	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
81	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
82	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
83	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
84	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
85	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
86	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
87	% there exists a finite filtration
88	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
89	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
90	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
91	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
92	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
93	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
94	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
95	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
96	\begin{proof}
97	
98	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case as a thin Mathlib wrapper.}
99	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself (i.e.\ \(M\) is a finite
100	  \(A\)-module, not merely a finite \(B\)-module). Since \(A\) is noetherian, a finite
101	  \(A\)-module is finitely presented over \(A\). Let \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) be the
102	  localisation of \(A\) at the multiplicative set \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\). The
103	  generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M = S^{-1} M\) is a \emph{finite-dimensional}
104	  \(K\)-vector space, hence free over \(K\); thus the freeness hypothesis at the
105	  generic localisation is automatic, with no input from the structure of \(B\). The
106	  result is then exactly Mathlib's
107	  \texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers}
108	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Free}): a finitely-presented \(A\)-module
109	  that is free over the localisation \(A_S = K\) becomes free over
110	  \texttt{Localization.Away}~\(r\) after inverting some \(r \in S\), i.e.\ some
111	  \(r \in A\), \(r \ne 0\). Setting \(f := r\) gives \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). (The
112	  openness of the free locus is the \texttt{Module.freeLocus} API of
113	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus}, which records that the locus
114	  where \(M\) is free over \(A\) is open and, by the above, non-empty.) This closes
115	  the theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
116	
117	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
118	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
119	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
120	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,

330	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
331	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
332	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
333	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
334	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
335	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
336	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
337	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
338	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
339	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
340	  free over \(A_f\).
341	\end{proof}

343	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
344	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
345	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
346	  \mathlibok
347	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
348	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
349	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
350	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
351	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
352	\end{lemma}
353	
354	\begin{lemma}
355	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
356	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
357	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
358	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
359	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
360	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
361	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
362	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
363	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
364	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
365	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
366	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
367	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
368	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
369	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
370	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
371	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
372	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
373	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
374	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
375	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
376	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
377	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
378	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
379	  %         p ∈ Set.range
380	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
381	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
382	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
383	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
384	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
385	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
386	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
387	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
388	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
389	\end{lemma}
390	
391	\begin{proof}
392	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
393	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
394	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
395	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
396	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
397	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
398	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
399	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
400	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
401	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
402	\end{proof}

452	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write

476	  % NOTE (iter-007 decomposition): the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
477	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
478	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
479	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
480	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
481	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
482	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
483	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
484	
485	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
486	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
487	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
488	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
489	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
490	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
491	
492	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
493	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
494	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
495	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
496	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
497	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
498	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
499	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
500	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
501	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
502	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
503	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
504	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
505	
506	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
507	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending
508	  \(X_j \mapsto b_j\); the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited
509	  from their independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the
510	  module-finiteness just established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the
511	  image of \(\varphi\).
512	\end{proof}
513	
514	\begin{lemma}
515	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
516	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
517	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
518	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
519	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
520	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
521	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
522	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
523	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
524	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
525	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
526	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
527	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
528	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
529	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
530	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
531	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
532	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
533	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
534	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
535	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
536	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
537	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
538	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
539	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
540	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
541	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
542	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
543	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
544	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
545	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
546	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
547	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
548	  \[
549	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
550	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
551	  \]
552	
553	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
554	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
555	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
556	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
557	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
558	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
559	\end{lemma}
560	
561	\begin{proof}
562	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
563	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
564	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
565	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
566	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
567	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
568	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
569	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
570	
571	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
572	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
573	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
574	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
575	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
576	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
577	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
578	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
579	
580	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
581	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
582	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
583	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
584	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
585	\end{proof}

614	  %         (_ : Module (Localization.Away g)

624	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
